title: Sorting kerbside recycling correctly
subtitle: What most councils accept, what contaminates a load, and how to check your area
excerpt: One contaminated bag can cause a lorry load to go to landfill rather than recycling. Accepted materials vary by council, but the most commonly refused items, black plastic trays, film plastic, and greasy cardboard, account for most household contamination. Recyclenow.com looks up accepted materials by postcode.
sourceNotes: WRAP / Recyclenow.com (gov-backed UK recycling lookup). WRAP UK Plastics Pact. Defra Household Waste and Recycling data.

content.text: Kerbside recycling gets sorted into material streams at a Materials Recovery Facility (MRF). The wrong item in the bin, or a recyclable that's too dirty, jams the sorting line and downgrades or ruins the whole load. The most useful thing you can do is know what your council accepts and what it does not.
Glass bottles and jars (any colour), rinsed.
Cardboard, flattened, not greasy. Pizza boxes are borderline: scrape off food, cut off the greasy base, recycle the clean lid.
Paper, newspapers, office paper, catalogues. Not shredded (shredded paper jams sorters in most facilities).
Plastic bottles with necks (milk, drinks, shampoo, cleaning products), plastic type 1 (PET) and type 2 (HDPE). Rinse.
Aluminium foil, clean (scrunch into a ball first so it doesn't blow off sorter belts).
Black plastic trays (ready meal, meat packaging), carbon black pigment blocks infrared sorters in most UK MRFs. Check if your council specifically accepts them.
Film plastic and carrier bags, jam conveyor belts. Return to supermarket collection points instead.
Polystyrene, not collected at kerbside by most councils.
Crisp packets and foil pouches, mixed materials, not separable at MRF.
Nappies, tissues, and kitchen roll, not recycled anywhere in the UK kerbside system.

Wishcycling is putting items in the recycling bin in the hope that they can be recycled, when they cannot. Common examples: mixed plastic films, electrical items, clothes, food-contaminated packaging. The effect is the same as deliberate contamination, the item creates processing problems. When in doubt, check recyclenow.com or put the item in general waste.

From 2025 onwards, English councils must collect the same core materials under the Simpler Recycling rules: glass, plastic bottles, tins, cartons, paper, and card. Plastic pots, tubs, and trays also become a must. Film plastic collection follows from 2027. When your council updates its service, check the website for the new list of accepted items.
